pattern: Aggressive sinus disease with bony destruction

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a destructive soft-tissue mass centered in the sinonasal cavity causing extensive erosion of the adjacent osseous structures, such as the nasal septum, turbinates, and medial maxillary sinus walls. The mass typically demonstrates soft-tissue attenuation on CT and appears hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted MRI sequences.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include mucosal thickening that progresses to a frank soft-tissue mass, which avidly enhances post-contrast. If concurrent chest imaging is available, there may be multiple thick-walled cavitating pulmonary nodules, which are highly characteristic of this systemic disease.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of hyperdense fungal secretions with internal calcifications to suggest invasive fungal sinusitis. Furthermore, there is no unilateral focal mass centered strictly within the maxillary antrum with gross asymmetric expansion to suggest a primary sinonasal carcinoma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the midline destructive sinonasal mass causing extensive cartilaginous and bony septal erosion, particularly if accompanied by cavitating pulmonary nodules or renal impairment.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Wegener granulomatosis, given the aggressive midline nasal septal destruction and T2-hypointense enhancing soft tissue mass. This necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis classically presents with recurrent sinonasal symptoms and can rapidly progress to extensive bony erosion.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Invasive fungal sinusitis, however this characteristically occurs in profoundly immunosuppressed or poorly controlled diabetic patients, rather than presenting as a primarily autoimmune vasculitis. Additionally, fungal sinusitis often demonstrates markedly hyperdense sinus contents on CT and profound T2 hypointensity due to fungal elements and heavy metals, which are not the predominant features here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Sinonasal carcinoma, given that squamous cell carcinoma is the most common malignancy in this region and missing it would drastically delay definitive oncological resection and radiotherapy. However, squamous cell carcinomas usually present as a unilateral, asymmetric, expansile soft tissue mass rather than a midline process centered on the nasal septum.
differentials.unifyingSummary: Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of a multisystem granulomatous vasculitis affecting the respiratory tract and kidneys.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the extent of the local bone destruction, specifically assessing for orbital, skull base, or intracranial extension to guide surgical biopsy and prevent imminent complications.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to track the progression from simple mucosal thickening to frank osteocartilaginous destruction, and to monitor the response to immunosuppressive therapy.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the paranasal sinuses and brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for differentiating the inflammatory soft tissue mass from retained secretions, evaluating dural enhancement, and detecting early cavernous sinus or orbital invasion.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for the characteristic enhancing soft tissue mass and perineural spread, alongside T2-weighted sequences to help distinguish the hypointense granulomatous tissue from hyperintense retained mucoid secretions.
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis is needed, I would specify an endoscopic biopsy targeting the active, enhancing margins of the nasal septal or sinus soft tissue mass to obtain histological confirmation of necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Rheumatology and Head & Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of septal and skull base destruction, any orbital involvement, and the status of pulmonary imaging to coordinate urgent systemic immunosuppressive treatment and localized surgical debridement.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a destructive soft-tissue mass centered in the sinonasal cavity causing extensive erosion of the adjacent osseous structures, such as the nasal septum, turbinates, and medial maxillary sinus walls. The mass typically demonstrates soft-tissue attenuation on CT and appears hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted MRI sequences. Supporting features include mucosal thickening that progresses to a frank soft-tissue mass, which avidly enhances post-contrast. If concurrent chest imaging is available, there may be multiple thick-walled cavitating pulmonary nodules, which are highly characteristic of this systemic disease. Importantly, there is no evidence of hyperdense fungal secretions with internal calcifications to suggest invasive fungal sinusitis. Furthermore, there is no unilateral focal mass centered strictly within the maxillary antrum with gross asymmetric expansion to suggest a primary sinonasal carcinoma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the midline destructive sinonasal mass causing extensive cartilaginous and bony septal erosion, particularly if accompanied by cavitating pulmonary nodules or renal impairment.

The appearances are most in keeping with Wegener granulomatosis, given the aggressive midline nasal septal destruction and T2-hypointense enhancing soft tissue mass. This necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis classically presents with recurrent sinonasal symptoms and can rapidly progress to extensive bony erosion. The principle differential would be Invasive fungal sinusitis, however this characteristically occurs in profoundly immunosuppressed or poorly controlled diabetic patients, rather than presenting as a primarily autoimmune vasculitis. Additionally, fungal sinusitis often demonstrates markedly hyperdense sinus contents on CT and profound T2 hypointensity due to fungal elements and heavy metals, which are not the predominant features here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Sinonasal carcinoma, given that squamous cell carcinoma is the most common malignancy in this region and missing it would drastically delay definitive oncological resection and radiotherapy. However, squamous cell carcinomas usually present as a unilateral, asymmetric, expansile soft tissue mass rather than a midline process centered on the nasal septum. Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of a multisystem granulomatous vasculitis affecting the respiratory tract and kidneys.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately map the extent of the local bone destruction, specifically assessing for orbital, skull base, or intracranial extension to guide surgical biopsy and prevent imminent complications. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to track the progression from simple mucosal thickening to frank osteocartilaginous destruction, and to monitor the response to immunosuppressive therapy. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the paranasal sinuses and brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for differentiating the inflammatory soft tissue mass from retained secretions, evaluating dural enhancement, and detecting early cavernous sinus or orbital invasion. I would specify T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for the characteristic enhancing soft tissue mass and perineural spread, alongside T2-weighted sequences to help distinguish the hypointense granulomatous tissue from hyperintense retained mucoid secretions. If tissue diagnosis is needed, I would specify an endoscopic biopsy targeting the active, enhancing margins of the nasal septal or sinus soft tissue mass to obtain histological confirmation of necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis. This should go to the Rheumatology and Head & Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of septal and skull base destruction, any orbital involvement, and the status of pulmonary imaging to coordinate urgent systemic immunosuppressive treatment and localized surgical debridement.
